description.s: Time-based reminders on items, with native OS notifications on Android and desktop (both via Tauri).
Architecture
Reminders are dedicated items, attached to their target items. Any item can have reminders via the attachment relationship. Multiple reminders per item are naturally supported.

Scheduling logic lives in userland. A userland library item wraps Tauri notification plugin calls. A boot-scan item runs on kernel:boot-complete to re-register alarms with the OS (handles reboots and process kills). The kernel has no knowledge of Tauri or notifications.

The native layer is a dumb translator — it receives timestamps and display text, schedules OS alarms, and shows notifications. All intelligence (recurrence, snooze, relative times) stays in userland Hob code.
Scheduling via Hob Expression
Each reminder item contains a Hob expression that answers one question: "when next?" The expression evaluates to either a timestamp (schedule it) or nil (don't schedule).
One-shot
— expression returns a fixed future timestamp; returns nil once dismissed.
Recurring
— expression computes the next occurrence from now (e.g., next weekday at 9am).
One-shot vs recurring is a property of the expression, not a separate system concept.
The scheduling loop: evaluate → got a timestamp? → schedule with native layer → alarm fires → wake WebView → re-evaluate → repeat. Nil means stop.

If the WebView can't be woken after an alarm fires (e.g., OEM battery optimization killed the process), the next occurrence won't be scheduled until the user opens the app and boot-scan runs. This is expected behavior — same degradation as device reboots.
Notification Behavior

When a notification is tapped, deep-link to the reminder item (not the target). The reminder item provides dismiss/snooze/reschedule actions and navigation to the target via the attachment relationship.
Android Gotchas
WebView JS is paused in background
— userland-only timers don't work on Android; native AlarmManager is required.
OEM battery optimization
— Xiaomi, Samsung, Huawei etc. aggressively kill background apps. Native AlarmManager has the best chance of surviving.
POST_NOTIFICATIONS permission
— required on Android 13+. Must handle denial gracefully.
SCHEDULE_EXACT_ALARM permission
— required on Android 12+ for exact alarms. Inexact alarms can be delayed by minutes or hours.
Alarms cleared on reboot
— boot-scan re-registers on next app open.
Tauri notification plugin
— @tauri-apps/plugin-notification supports scheduling in 2.x. Check whether it handles boot persistence natively or if a custom Rust/Kotlin plugin is needed.
Implementation Sketch
Create a reminder type with a Hob expression field for scheduling
Build a view for reminder items (date/time picker, expression editor, dismiss/snooze actions)
Build a userland library item wrapping Tauri notification plugin calls
On reminder save: evaluate expression, invoke Tauri command to schedule/cancel the native alarm
On boot (kernel:boot-complete): scan reminder items, evaluate expressions, re-register active alarms
When alarm fires: native side shows notification and wakes WebView; WebView re-evaluates for next occurrence
Notification tap: deep-link to reminder item via ?item=GUID
Priority
Low — useful for personal productivity but requires Tauri-native plugin work (Rust/Kotlin). The userland scheduling logic is straightforward; the hard part is the Android integration.
